onlinePresenceArticles.article:
The people making hiring decisions *will* Google you. This means:

1. Yes, they will form their first impression of you before you meet.
2. AND this impression will depend on all the information Google surfaces when they type in your name.

Today, Google is a hirer's entryway into who you are.

And that makes it a great place to begin. This guide will walk you through cataloging all the pages, images, and videos that show up for the keyword `{YOUR_NAME}`.

**Resource:** [This Google sheet](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1iVlBUQag3fZNkDt2P1xh2vpSh6RSwoA1AZIRxrFRACM/copy?usp=sharing) is where we will be doing the actual cataloging. Keep it handy, it will serve as your source of truth through this stage and next three. Owning your online presence is as simple as getting each page in this sheet to a place you can be proud of.

We are going to break this down into a 3 step process that you can get started working on today:

## **Step 1**: Keyword: `{YOUR_NAME}`

Open up your [catalog Google sheet](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1iVlBUQag3fZNkDt2P1xh2vpSh6RSwoA1AZIRxrFRACM/copy?usp=sharing).

In parallel, turn on your VPN, open up a private / incognito window, and Google your name.

We're going to record the following:

1. **Keyword:** The keyword you're using — in this case, `{YOUR_NAME}`.
2. **Page:** List out the pages that show up for your keyword.
3. **Position (All):** Make a note of each page's position on the "All" tab. The first three will usually have a higher clickthrough rate than the rest of the links combined (definitely something we'll look into later on). Record the pages that show up for positions 1 to 15 (safe to assume no one's going to get past the second page of search results).
4. **Position (Images):** Google populates the "Images" tab with images from the pages that contain your name. Click on the "Images" tab and record where the images are coming from. If you that an image is from a page you haven't listed as yet — add it in.
5. **Position (Videos):** Do the same for the "Videos" tab.
6. **Who owns this page?** Make a note of who this page belongs to. If it's your personal website  and you have control over all the content, type in your own name. If you have to interface with an app or a team to change the content, type in theirs. It's a good idea to know upfront what kind of effort you need to put into updating each of these pages to better reflect who you are.

## Step 2: Related keywords

`{YOUR_NAME}` might not be the only keyword that you want to look up.

If you share your name will other people, type in a more specific keyword like `{YOUR_NAME}` `{YOUR_HOMETOWN}` or `{YOUR_NAME}` `{YOUR_FAVORITE_FRAMEWORK}`.

If you own a lot of content online, check out Google's list of related keywords at the bottom of the page and give those a whirl.

Repeat step 1 will each related keyword. (Remember not to catalog the same page twice though.)

### Remember

**Your catalog *will* surprise you.** Even seasoned professionals who spend a lot of time optimizing their search results are bewildered to find that

- Google sometimes pulls an image from a page that's six years old.
- Or their alternate Twitter account is showing up next to their primary one.
- Or a page they created and forgot about is showing up in position three.

Don't worry, though, this is precisely why we're doing this. We're looking at your profile and polishing it up before the people who matter get here.

## What to do if none of these pages are yours

Maybe you share a name with a celebrity. Maybe your last name also happens to be a famous brand in Spain (it happens). Maybe you've lived on a deserted island for the last two decades and this is the first time you're using the internet.

Don't panic! Here's how you can carve out a (virtual) space to call your own if `{YOUR_NAME}` is already taken:

- **Optimize for a more specific keyword.** If "Katie Smith" is already taken, you could optimize for (i.e., refer to yourself as) "Kat M. Smith" or "KM Smith". You could also go in a different direction: "Katie Smith Oregon" (if you live in Oregon) or "Katie Smith React" (if you want to be known for a certain kind of work).
- **Choose a unique username.** You could also go by your username, a lot of people do! "Katercoder" or "Katersmith" don't come off as any less professional on the interwebs.
- **Skip to stage 2.** If you really have a dearth of content to call your own, skip this step altogether and move on to stage 2. You can revisit this stage once you've got a few social media profiles set up.
- **Focus on SEO.** This is a time-consuming strategy — but worth it if you're up for the effort — you could compete with everyone else optimizing for "your name." This means really drilling down to on-page SEO and creating enough content to signal to Google that *you* are the foremost authority on the keyword `{YOUR_NAME}`.

### Pro-tip

**Add in other categories you'd like to track as separate columns.** For example: you might want your work-related pages to show up before your hobby-related pages. In this case, it's definitely worth tagging work pages in a separate column so you can optimize them first.

## Step 3: How do I start updating my pages?

You're ready for the next stage! Keep your online presence catalog handy and ready to move on to the next stage, where we will help you figure out how to answer this question.
onlinePresenceArticles.title: Breaking Down Your Online Presence

onlinePresenceArticles.article:
## **Who are we updating your pages for???**

That's a question we need to answer to figure out whether:

1. Your page needs an update to begin with and
2. What kind of information we use to make the update.

In this stage, we'll spend some time working through your preferences in the workplace. If you're clear on the kind of workplace that best suits you, can you tailor your Google search results to stand out decision makes there?

### Note

We're *not* trying to make a list of companies you should start applying to. We *are* trying to figure out what kind of professional environments you gravitate towards as a person.

The point of this stage is to help you reflect on:

1. The kind of company you want to work for and,
2. What's most important to you in a work environment (which will be useful *long after* this project is complete.)

## Reflecting on culture

A company's culture is a milieu too vast for even the science-based personality test. These tests are sold as navigational compasses — instruments that will lay out a map of how well you will fit within the milieu — but they're just a step above reading tea leaves. And they don't help you think about what matters most to *you*.

Values on the company wall aside (teamwork, Inclusion — you know what usually goes on the wall), culture is influenced by 1) how fast the company moves and 2) how autonomous the people moving it are encouraged to be.

![your desired pace and autonomy as a 2x2 grid](https://res.cloudinary.com/dg3gyk0gu/image/upload/v1620926086/online-presence/pace-and-autonomy.jpg)

**Source:** This chart is adapted from Spencer Stuart's [Integrated Culture](https://www.spencerstuart.com/-/media/2020/may/hbr_leaders_guide_corporate_culture_updated.pdf) framework. Where Stuart focuses more on managers and leaders in a company, our chart accounts for how his tenets might change from an employee's POV. You can check out HBR's writeup on corporate culture [here](https://hbr.org/2018/01/the-leaders-guide-to-corporate-culture).

Here are some questions to consider as you decide what balance of these you're comfortable with:

1. **On a scale of one to ten, how predictable do you like your day-to-day to be?** (1 — I enjoy it when every day looks the same. 10 — I like it when I can't tell what tomorrow will look like.)
2. **On a scale of one to ten, how much guidance do you enjoy when working with a problem?** ( 1 — the more my manager tells me what to do the better. 10 — I'm perfectly comfortable with a hands-off approach.)

## Reflecting on the nature of the work

![your comfort zone versus the desirability of work context as a 2x2 grid](https://res.cloudinary.com/dg3gyk0gu/image/upload/v1620926084/online-presence/comfort-zone-versus-work-context-desireability.jpg)

Now that you've considered the working conditions, consider the work itself. How do you like to learn? What kind of projects do you like to work on? How comfortably can you handle being outside of your comfort zone? How would you react if you want to work on `project` but the company needed `system maintenance`?

Again, it's a balance. Every role comes with challenges, and every learning comes with discomfort. You want to figure out where your threshold for both are.

Here are some questions to consider as you decide what balance of these you're comfortable with:

1. **On a scale of 1 to 10, are you comfortable with delaying work that you really want to do in order to do some work that is not exciting but the company needs?** (1 — I only want to do work that I like. 10 — I only want to do work that the company needs)
2. **On a scale of 1 to 10, how much you enjoy learning in a high-pressure environment?** (1 — I can't function outside my comfort zone, let alone learn. 10 — If there's no pressure, the lessons don't sink in.)

## Reflecting on your life goals

The final factor to consider is what your life goals are and how a company might be able to help you achieve them.

Your goal could be to retire in your forties, or build a reputation in the industry, or only do socially conscious work. Either way, your goals 1) impact the kind of companies you want to attract and 2) tell you where you can make compromises and where you can't.

## What kind of company do you want to work for?

For now, let's use the culture chart/questions to figure out what tone you should be taken when updating the pages in your G sheet.

At the moment, the goal is simply to make sure to use language that speaks to the right people.

### The options:

- **Fast-paced and autonomous:** Think about the early-stage startup environment. Things move very fast, you're racing towards profitability and trying to land those first few customers.
- **Fast-paced and guidance dependent:** Think about a start-up building traction. Established product-market fit, ample funding, and a growing team. Could also be a fast-moving enterprise company like Stripe or Intercom.
- **Steady-paced and guidance dependent:** late-stage start-up/enterprise company. Established playbooks, clear internal systems.
- **Steady-paced and autonomous:** Small team-driven companies, bootstrapped startups, open-source companies. Think about companies where sustainability is prioritized overgrowth (rare but they exist).

## What you need to starting setting the tone of your online presence in the next step

Use your reflections here to make a shortlist of 5 companies that you would love to work at. Don't worry if they have open positions or not, we're just trying to figure out how they communicate so that you can take an appealing tone in your online profile. Remember: you're not applying to the companies on this list, you're applying to other companies *like* them.

Here are a few guiding questions to help:

- What services and applications do you use and love?
- Which ones do you see potential in that you wish you could improve?
- Think about some of the experiences in your day-to-day, which of those do you find yourself thinking about in-depth?
- What do you like to learn about? Are there companies doing interesting work in that space?

Think across sectors, industries, and areas of specialization — you're looking to find the intersection between 1) what's available out there 2) the kind of work environment you thrive in and 3) the kind of skills you have to offer.

Document your list of companies (with any notes and ideas that occur as you're thinking about them) in your [trusty Google sheet](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1iVlBUQag3fZNkDt2P1xh2vpSh6RSwoA1AZIRxrFRACM/copy?usp=sharing).

## Choosing The Tone of Your Online Presence

Now that you have a rough idea of the *kind* of company you want to attract and impress with your online profile, it's time to start thinking about the *how* of it. 

In this stage, we will zero in on on the tone you need to take when tailoring material for the kind of company you have in mind. The work you do here will set you up to actually start making changes in Stage 3. 

## Why choose a tone?

If you compare the career pages of an enterprise company like [Stripe](https://stripe.com/in/jobs) or [Microsoft](https://careers.microsoft.com/professionals/us/en/c-engineering), and those from smaller companies like [Wistia](https://wistia.com/jobs) or [HelpScout](https://www.helpscout.com/company/careers/), you'll notice some stark differences.  

One of those big difference is the people-focus.

Microsoft's career page shouts "How can you contribute to our culture?" where Wistia's gently asks, "How can we celebrate who you are?" 

This matters. Especially when it comes to making a good first impression. If you're applying to a Microsoft, you want your online presence to come off a completely different way than if you were applying to a Wistia. 

People making decisions usually skim through information when they're googling you. So you could use the tiny amount of space in your Twitter bio to talk about playing the trumpet or excelling at React. Microsoft would care about the React. Wistia would care about both.

Check out the careers pages, marketing material, job descriptions, and Glassdoor reviews for the the five companies you've laid out at the top of this page — how would you like to present yourself to them? 

## General tone pointers

In either case, 

- **Write the way you talk.** This is underrated advice. In Edward Bailey's words, "Imagine you're actually standing in front of your reader. Or talking to them on the telephone. What would you say—in an organized and polite way? Write those words. Simply talk on the paper."
- **Think about layout as much as you think about words.** Bullet points instead of paragraphs, short paragraphs instead of long ones, lots of headers and visual breaks.
- **Use ordinary words.** Another way to put this is: be simple and clear. Simplicity and clarity beat artful, and beautiful language in every arena except the novel.
- **Use a variety of punctuation.** Commas, em dashes, hyphens, ellipses. The better you can capture the natural rhythm of speech, the better you writing will sound. Literally—your readers have a little voice in their heads reading your sentences out to them in the same way that a little voice is reading this one to you.
- **Be inclusive.** Use "they/them" pronouns more, or "folks" over "guys." You don't know who will be reading your work, and you don't want anyone reading your work to feel like you aren't talking to them.

**Useful guide for general guidelines:** Edward Bailey's [Plain English at Work](http://amazon.com/Plain-English-Work-Writing-Speaking/dp/0195104498) 

Above and beyond those basic rules, however, here's how to tailor your message: 

## Personality-first

**Key:** Focus on being authentic.

### Zero in on a  "core message"

The biggest challenge in personality-first messaging is focus. The better you deliver a consistent core message, the easier it is for people to remember who you are. 

Here are Jessica Zweig's guiding questions to help you navigate this space: 

- What are the things I care the most about?
- What are the things I could talk about endlessly?
- What are the things that demonstrate my expertise and my humanity?
- Do these topics enhance and elevate my professional expertise or detract from it?

(Read her book *["Be"](https://www.amazon.com/Be-No-Bullsh-Increasing-Simply-Yourself/dp/1683646991#:~:text=Jessica%20Zweig%20founded%20the%20SimplyBe,connection%20into%20your%20winning%20brand.)* for the full framework) 

The idea is to answer these questions in as much detail as you can and then whittle down to the few topics that you really believe in and want to put out into the world. 

Keep this message in mind as you revisit your pages (from the google sheet) and start making updates.

### Document your answers to the questions above here

- The things I care about most are...
- ...
- ...
- 

## Company-first

**Key:** Focus on the company. Showcase the parts of yourself that would appeal to them.

 In this case, you're taking messaging cues from the company you're applying to rather than yourself. Neither approach is better than the other, they're just different, and a personal choice. 

- What company values stand out to you?
- How does the company demonstrate its own work? (Take cues from its engineering blog, for example, or from writing where the company is learning in public)
- Check out the companies "About" and "Careers" pages — how do they look at their journey from founding to present?
- Check out some of the Glassdoor reviews of the company.

### Document your answers to the questions above here

- The kind of companies I'm looking at appreciate...
- ...
- ...
- ...
-
onlinePresenceArticles.title: Workplace Culture and the Tone of Your Online Presence

onlinePresenceArticles.article:
Now that you know *how* to update your pages, you're ready to revisit them! Make sure to update the following as you go through each page:

## 1. Bio

Take your bio through your tone/message pointers from stage three. Here are a few questions to think about as you do: 

- What can you do to make your bio more readable?
- How can you better lead with value?
- If you're taking a personality-first approach, how aligned is your bio with your core message?
- If you're taking a company-first approach, how well does your bio speak to what the company is looking for?

**Pro-tip:** If the stage 2 reflections on company culture and work environment helped you get some clarity on what kind of company you're looking for, include that information when you're updating your pages. Adding in a line like this — "I'm looking for a role at a well-established late-stage startup or enterprise company. I like order, clear internal systems, and working with reliable teams to achieve ambitious company goals" — can be extremely useful to people making hiring decisions. 

## 2. Personal information

If you're taking a personality-first approach, make sure that any personal information that shows up on these pages is consistent with your core message. 

If you're taking a company-first approach, on the other hand, remember to either delete information that's too personal. Alternatively, give it a positive spin — how has experimenting with art, for example, made you a better engineer? The idea isn't to downplay what makes you unique but to signal to the company how your unique interests will make you a better asset. 

## 3. Skills

Delete things that aren't necessary, particularly when it comes to your skills. Make sure that you're painting a relevant picture.

## 4. Images

Make sure your images are professional and up to date. 

Here's a great guide on how to choose the right picture for your LinkedIn / social media: [https://business.linkedin.com/talent-solutions/blog/2014/12/5-tips-for-picking-the-right-linkedin-profile-picture](https://business.linkedin.com/talent-solutions/blog/2014/12/5-tips-for-picking-the-right-linkedin-profile-picture) 

## 5. Information you might not be proud of

This is a good opportunity to revisit any information that doesn't help you make a good impression. Remember that Google might display your most popular tweets or pull pictures from your social media. Delete anything that might get in way of your coming across as professional.
onlinePresenceArticles.title: The 5-Point Basic Cleanup of Your Online Presence

onlinePresenceArticles.article:
The best way to enhance your online presence is to demonstrate your work more.  

Remember, though: Leveling up your online game isn't about building quantity, it's about quality. Use your "ideal work environment" learnings from stage 2 as your north star. How can create high quality work that will stand out to the companies on that list? 

## Create your personal site

If you don't already have a site on your own domain now's the time to consider it.

As a developer you are learning, researching, and implementing solutions on a daily basis. Capturing that experience and your notes is a deeply rewarding habit to practice.

Not only does this help you grow and learn by more deeply connecting your work in your mind while providing rich personal reference material, but it also shares your work with others. It allows potential collaborators the opportunity to learn more about you and the quality of your work.

To maximize the value of your effort, these posts should be posted to your own domain with your name associated with it.

One of our favorite ways to create a blog space that is really great to work with is using Eleventy.

⭐ [Check out this 20min free Eleventy course from Eleventy expert Stephanie Eckles](https://egghead.io/courses/build-an-eleventy-11ty-site-from-scratch-bfd30)

## Add resources to your site

Creating useful resources for yourself and others can take many shapes and forms. Many people enjoy varying the medium, or better yet presenting resources across several mediums.

A quick note or tweet can become a blog post. That blog post can serve as the basis for a livestream. The livestream can be expanded into an edited screencast that gets embedded in the original article and added to the thread on Twitter.

Setting aside a couple of hours a week to capture your research and solutions will pay compound interest over your career as a developer.

### Decide what to create

But don't spend too much time trying to decide. When in doubt **start writing**

⭐ [Here's how to write your first draft in two hours or less](https://egghead.io/own-your-online-presence/step-by-step-blog-post).

Here at egghead we **love** screencasts and have [created a screencast course on creating screencasts](https://egghead.io/courses/record-badass-screencasts-for-egghead-io)

⭐ [Try screencasting yourself solving a problem](https://howtoegghead.com/instructor/) and post it to YouTube.

⭐ [Learn how to setup a livestream on Twitch or other platforms](https://egghead.io/courses/how-to-livestream-code-and-design-on-twitch-6646).

## Revisit your best resources and expand them

- If you write a blog post, convert the highlights into a twitter thread
- If you give a talk, record a version for YouTube
- Curate your best resources and share them along with your favorite resources from others on social media

## Create new profiles to showcase your work

There are many places that you can start showcasing your work! Here are a few that we like: 

- dev.to
- stackoverflow
- codesandbox
- codepen
- glitch
- egghead
onlinePresenceArticles.title: Level Up Your Online Presence

onlinePresenceArticles.article:
Writing a blog is easier than you think! Most follow the same basic structure. Answer the questions here to start off with a solid first draft. And remember to write the way you talk! 

Example: [https://jvns.ca/blog/2016/03/29/thread-pools-part-ii-i-love-blocking/](https://jvns.ca/blog/2016/03/29/thread-pools-part-ii-i-love-blocking/)

## Part 1: _Intro_

- What are problems are you going to talk about in this post?
- Why was it a problem for you? Why were you stuck with it?
- Did you manage to find a solution?
- If there's one main idea you'd like readers to walk away with, what would that idea be?

## Part 2: _Problem_

- How does _bigger technology that problem fits into_ work? Give readers some context.
- What led you to realize that _problem_ was a problem to begin with?
- What do people need to understand about _bigger technology_ to understand _problem_ better? (Is there an analogy that helped you think about _problem_ better? **Example:** _problem_ works just like a...)
- How did you try solving _problem_ before you hit on _solution_? (Every failure helped you learn, so don't be afraid to talk about how things went wrong)
- How did you feel as you were trying to solve _problem_?

## Part 3: _Solution_

- When did your journey with _problem_ take a turn for the better?
- How did you feel when you started making progress?
- Explain the details of _solution_ (Be as explicit as possible about *how* the solution works or what it *does* to the system)
- How did you understanding of _bigger technology_ change in light of _solution_?

## Part 4: _Results_

- How did _solution_ impact your project? What was different after you solved _problem_?
- What are your biggest takeaways from the process?
- What's the first thing you'll do when you encounter _problem_ again?
- How has your understanding of _bigger technology_ changed now that you've solved _problem_?
- How does _solution_ relate to the _big idea_ in the introduction.

## Part 5: _Conclusion_

- In a line, tell readers what problem you were trying to solve.
- In a line, tell readers how you solved it.
- What are you going to work on next?

---

If you've answered these questions, congratulations, you have the first draft! All you need to do now is polish, polish, polish. 

Here's how. 

### The only editing tips you really need:

- **Read your post out loud (or even to a real person).** Are there any sentences that feel clunky, long, or don't make too much sense? Those are the sentences you need to fix.
- **Actively try to cut out words.** No one likes to admit this but this is 80% of editing. Go through each sentence and try to say the same thing is fewer words.
- **Make a list of supporting ideas.** One of the first things we did as a part of this exercise was to come with *one* main idea to leave your readers with. How did you support this idea? Make a flowchart of how you introduced and communicated this idea through your post and you've got yourself the logical flow of the narrative. Are there supporting ideas you can add? Or ones you can remove?
onlinePresenceArticles.title: How to Write a Blog Post Step by Step
